title: How Not to Save the Planet
gist: Alan Jacobs argues that modern environmental writing abandoned the older 'topophilia' tradition of loving attention to a single particular place in favor of a hectoring, statistics-driven 'Big Picture' rhetoric of rage and doom that can only reach the already-converted; he contends that 'global thinking' is psychologically depressing and politically ineffective, and that effective conservation flows from local affection, not abstract alarm.

insights.claim: Effective nature writing historically belonged to a 'topophilia' tradition—loving, detailed attention to one small particular place (Thoreau's pond, Dillard's creek, Leopold's county)—motivated by affection rather than alarm.
insights.speaker: Alan Jacobs
insights.so_what: Reframes persuasion as a function of love-of-the-particular, not data volume—relevant to how any cause builds durable public support.
insights.quote: these writers show to us the sensual properties of one dear place

insights.claim: Jonathan Schell's 1982 'The Fate of the Earth' established a new tradition that rejected place-specificity for the 'Big Picture' of Earth and Nature conveyed by statistics and charts, motivated by frustration and rage rather than love.
insights.speaker: Alan Jacobs
insights.so_what: Identifies the origin point of the doom-and-data genre that now dominates—useful for diagnosing why climate messaging often fails to move people.
insights.quote: Schell had effectively established a new tradition of nature writing, one that rejected the specificity of topophilia in favor of the Big Picture.
insights.locator: Schell had effectively established a new tradition

insights.claim: Books that open by presuming readers are ignorant—David Wallace-Wells's 'It is worse, much worse, than you think'—adopt a hectoring rhetoric that can reach only those whose anger already matches the author's, enough for bestseller lists but not enough to change outcomes.
insights.speaker: Alan Jacobs
insights.so_what: Direct critique of preaching-to-the-converted: commercial success can mask political ineffectiveness—a caution for advocacy and ESG messaging alike.
insights.quote: such books can reach only those whose anger already matches that of the authors
insights.locator: My concern is that such books

insights.claim: Abstract statistics about 'the Earth' or 'Nature' fail to move the 'slow, unreckoning heart,' which needs something smaller and concrete to engage its power of affection—per Richard Wilbur, people are 'unable to fear what is too strange.'
insights.speaker: Alan Jacobs
insights.so_what: Psychological mechanism for why scale-of-the-problem framing backfires; emotional engagement requires graspable specifics.
insights.quote: Our slow, unreckoning hearts will be left behind, / Unable to fear what is too strange.
insights.locator: the poet Richard Wilbur ... 'Advice to a Prophet'

insights.claim: Wendell Berry argued that 'global thinking' is not really possible—those who claim it (most successfully imperial governments and multinational corporations) rely on extreme, oppressive simplifications, making global thinkers 'dangerous people' who are intrinsically destructive of actual places.
insights.speaker: Alan Jacobs
insights.so_what: A sharp critique of top-down planetary management; warns that grand abstraction enables large-scale harm—relevant to debates over centralized vs. local solutions.
insights.quote: Global thinkers have been, and will be, dangerous people.

insights.claim: When global thought is not actively destructive it tends to induce depression in those who attempt it, accounting for the gloomy, finger-wagging tone of contemporary environmental writing.
insights.speaker: Alan Jacobs
insights.so_what: Links a rhetorical style to a psychological cost—suggests doomism is self-reinforcing and demoralizing rather than mobilizing.
insights.quote: global thought ... tends to encourage depression in those who attempt it

insights.claim: John Stuart Mill's mental crisis showed that achieving all one's reform objectives can bring no joy when happiness was tied only to the pursuit, and that 'the habit of analysis has a tendency to wear away the feelings' when not balanced by cultivated affection.
insights.speaker: Alan Jacobs
insights.so_what: Frames purely analytical, goal-driven activism as emotionally hollowing—argues feeling must be cultivated alongside reasoning.
insights.quote: the habit of analysis has a tendency to wear away the feelings
insights.locator: the nineteenth-century philosopher John Stuart Mill

insights.claim: Pains and pleasures forcibly associated with causes by reward and punishment (operant conditioning) lack any natural tie; durable motivation arises only from direct experience and affection, which Mill recovered through reading poetry.
insights.speaker: Alan Jacobs
insights.so_what: Argues incentive-and-shame approaches to behavior change are shallow versus intrinsic, affection-rooted motivation—applies to any campaign relying on guilt.
insights.quote: are not connected with them by any natural tie

insights.claim: Jacobs's thesis: to save the planet, forget The Planet—learn to love one dear pond, creek, or valley, because obligations arise from deepest affections and what you love, others will learn to love and conserve.
insights.speaker: Alan Jacobs
insights.so_what: Offers a concrete alternative theory of change for conservation—bottom-up affection over top-down alarm; reframes how environmental engagement could actually scale.
insights.quote: If you would save the planet, forget The Planet; if you would sustain and repair nature, forget Nature.
